teaching.title: Comparison Operators in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ inherits C's comparison system but adds a crucial upgrade: a proper bool type that prints 'true' and 'false'. The same six judges (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=) deliver verdicts, but now they speak in bool, not just 0 and 1.
teaching.content: C++ comparison operators (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=) return bool: true or false — not 1 and 0 like in C. C++ has native bool, making code more readable and type-safe than C.

examples.title: C++ Comparisons Return bool
examples.explanation: Native bool — more readable than C's 0/1.

examples.title: String Comparison in C++
examples.explanation: C++ string uses == correctly — unlike Java!

teaching.funFact: C++ std::string overloads the == operator to compare content — unlike Java's String. This is C++ operator overloading at work. You can define what == does for your own classes too! 🔧

teaching.title: Logical Operators in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ supports BOTH the C-style operators (&&, ||, !) AND the English keyword alternatives (and, or, not). The keyword versions were added for readability and to avoid confusion with bitwise operators. Most professionals still use &&, ||, !.
teaching.content: C++ has && (and), || (or), ! (not) — same as C. C++ also adds keyword alternatives: 'and', 'or', 'not' which are standard C++ keywords (not common in practice). Short-circuit evaluation applies.

examples.title: Logical Operators

teaching.funFact: C++ is the only mainstream language that has BOTH symbol (&&, ||) AND English keyword (and, or, not) logical operators. The keyword versions were standardized in C++98 to improve readability! 📚

teaching.title: Arithmetic & auto In Expressions
teaching.analogy: C++ arithmetic works like C's, but with a modern twist: auto can deduce the type of a result. Combined with C++'s stronger type safety, you get power WITH protection — less likely to accidentally mix types in silent ways.
teaching.content: C++ arithmetic is identical to C's (+, -, *, /, %). Integer division still truncates. Compound operators work (+=, -=, etc.). C++ adds auto for result-type deduction and is stricter about implicit conversions.

examples.title: Arithmetic with auto
examples.explanation: auto deduces the right type from the expression.
examples.code:
auto x = 10;         // int
auto y = 3;          // int
auto result = x / y; // int! 10/3 = 3 (truncates)
auto ratio = 10.0 / 3; // double! one double → both promoted

cout << result << endl; // 3
cout << ratio << endl;  // 3.33333

examples.title: C++ type mixing rules
examples.explanation: Mixing int and double promotes to double.

teaching.funFact: C++11's auto makes type deduction a core feature. Combined with 'decltype', you can write code where the compiler figures out ALL types. This enables powerful generic programming! 🚀

mcq.q: In C++, string s1='hi', s2='hi'. Does (s1 == s2) return true?
options: Yes — C++ string overloads == for content comparison
mcq.explanation: C++ String ==: Unlike Java where String == compares references, C++ std::string overloads the == operator to compare CONTENT. (s1 == s2) correctly returns true if both have the same characters.

mcq.q: What is the C++ keyword alternative to '&&'?
mcq.explanation: C++ Keywords: C++ provides 'and', 'or', 'not' as alternatives to &&, ||, !. They're standard C++ but rarely used in practice. Most code uses && because it's familiar from C.

mcq.q: auto result = 7 / 2. What is result's type and value?
mcq.explanation: Auto + Int Division: 7 and 2 are both int literals → integer division → 3 (truncated). auto deduces result as int with value 3, not 3.5.

mcq.q: C++ bool prints as what by default (without boolalpha)?
mcq.explanation: Bool Output: Without boolalpha, cout prints bool as 1 (true) or 0 (false). Add 'cout << boolalpha;' to make it print 'true'/'false' as words.

mcq.q: int a = 5; a *= 3; a -= 2; What is a?
mcq.explanation: Step by step: a = 5. a *= 3 → a = 15. a -= 2 → a = 13. Output: 13.

coding.title: C++ String Comparison
coding.objective: In C++, how do you correctly compare two string variables? Use operator.
coding.starterCode:
string a = "hello", b = "hello";
if (a ___ b) cout << "same";
coding.hint: C++ string overloads == for content comparison — unlike Java!

coding.title: Auto Division
coding.hint: Both are int literals → integer division → 9÷4 = 2 remainder 1.

coding.title: Bool with boolalpha
coding.objective: How do you make cout print 'true' instead of '1' for booleans?
coding.hint: Stream manipulator that switches to alphabetical bool output.

options: .equals() is a Java method. In C++, use: if (name == "Alex") — C++ string == compares content
debugging.answer: .equals() is a Java method. In C++, use: if (name == "Alex") — C++ string == compares content
debugging.hint: C++ std::string overloads == for content comparison. No .equals() method exists in C++.

prediction.explanation: true && false = false = 0. true || false = true = 1. !false = true = 1. Output: 0↵1↵1.

teaching.title: if / else if / else in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ if/else is identical in structure to C and Java — they all copied from the same source. But C++ brings its own upgrade: 'if constexpr' for compile-time decisions, and C++17's 'if with initializer' for clean scoping.
teaching.content: C++ if/else syntax is identical to C and Java: condition in (), blocks in {}. C++ requires the condition to be convertible to bool (0/null = false, everything else = true — same as C). Modern C++17 adds 'if init-statements'.

examples.title: Standard C++ if/else
examples.explanation: Same as C and Java structurally.

examples.title: C++17: if with initializer
examples.explanation: Declare and test in one line — cleaner scope.

teaching.funFact: C++17's 'if init-statement' was inspired by Python's assignment expressions (walrus operator :=). Two very different languages, same idea: compute and test in one step! 🔄

teaching.title: Range-Based for & if constexpr
teaching.analogy: if constexpr is a superpower unique to C++: it makes branching decisions AT COMPILE TIME, not at runtime. Imagine if your GPS could plan the route BEFORE you even started driving, eliminating impossible paths entirely.
teaching.content: C++17 'if constexpr' evaluates the condition at compile time — the false branch is completely excluded from compiled code. This is used in template metaprogramming. Regular if evaluates at runtime.

examples.title: Range-based for (preview)
examples.explanation: C++'s cleaner loop syntax — great with decisions.

teaching.funFact: if constexpr is so powerful it can remove entire functions from the binary at compile time — programs get smaller AND faster. Used heavily in modern C++ template libraries like STL and Boost! 📦

mcq.q: C++ if/else syntax compared to C's if/else:
options: C++ if/else syntax is identical to C's
mcq.explanation: Identical Syntax: C++ if/else syntax is exactly the same as C's — condition in (), blocks in {}. Java also copied this from C. Only Python differs (no parens, colon required, indentation-based).

mcq.q: In C++, can an int be used as an if condition?
options: Yes — 0 is false, non-zero is true (same as C)
mcq.explanation: Inherited from C: C++ accepts any scalar as condition — 0/nullptr/false = false, everything else = true. This C heritage makes C++ more flexible than Java which requires strict bool.

mcq.q: What is the benefit of C++17 'if init-statement'?
options: Restricts the initialized variable's scope to the if block
mcq.explanation: Scoped Init: if (int x = compute(); x > 0) { } limits x to the if-block scope. Without this, you'd declare x before the if and it would linger in the outer scope unnecessarily.

coding.title: Grade Branch
coding.objective: int grade = 75. if>=90→A, else if>=80→B, else if>=70→C else F. Output?
coding.hint: 75 is not >=90 or >=80, but IS >=70 → C.

coding.title: C++ uses bool
coding.objective: bool isOpen = true; Does if (isOpen) run? yes or no
coding.hint: isOpen is true → the if block runs.

options: Missing semicolon at end of cout line — every C++ statement needs ;
debugging.answer: Missing semicolon at end of cout line — every C++ statement needs ;
debugging.hint: Every statement in C++ ends with ;. The cout line is missing its semicolon.

prediction.explanation: x=15. 15>20 false. 15>10 true → 'medium'. Once matched, rest skipped.

teaching.title: switch in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ switch inherits all of C's switch behavior — int/char only, fall-through, break required. But C++17 adds [[fallthrough]] to document intentional fall-through, making code self-explanatory instead of bug-prone.
teaching.content: C++ switch works identically to C: int and char types, cases must be constants, break prevents fall-through, default is catch-all. C++17 adds [[fallthrough]] attribute for intentional fall-through and a cleaner switch-init syntax.

examples.title: C++ switch
examples.explanation: Same as C — int/char, break required.

examples.title: C++17 [[fallthrough]] attribute

teaching.funFact: C++17 also introduced 'switch with initializer' just like C++17 if: switch (auto val = compute(); val % 2) { ... } — the val is scoped to the switch block! 🆕

teaching.title: Ternary & Structured Bindings
teaching.analogy: The ternary operator is C++'s Swiss Army knife for one-line decisions. In C++11 and later, you can even use ternary with auto to let the compiler figure out the return type — making it more flexible than in C.
teaching.content: C++ ternary: condition ? expr1 : expr2. Works with all types. In C++, both branches must return compatible types (stricter than C). C++ also allows ternary as an lvalue in some contexts.

examples.title: Ternary in C++

teaching.funFact: C++ ternary can return references (lvalues), meaning you can use it on the LEFT side of an assignment: (a > b ? a : b) = 0; — sets the larger of a or b to 0! This is impossible in Java or Python! 🤯

mcq.q: C++ switch supports which types? (same as C)
mcq.explanation: Integer Types Only: C++ switch (like C) only works with integer types: int, char, short, long, and enums. C++ string is not supported — use if/else chains for string switching.

options: Documents that fall-through is intentional — suppresses compiler warning
mcq.explanation: [[fallthrough]]: This C++17 attribute tells the compiler 'I know this falls through — it's intentional'. Suppresses the -Wimplicit-fallthrough warning. Better than a comment because it's machine-readable.

mcq.q: auto result = (x > 0) ? 10 : 20.0. If x=5, what is result's type?
mcq.explanation: Type Promotion: Ternary's two branches are 10 (int) and 20.0 (double). When mixed, both are promoted to the common type: double. result is double. This is stricter than C.

coding.title: C++ switch break
coding.objective: What prevents fall-through in a C++ switch case?
coding.hint: Same as C and Java — break stops execution at the end of a case.

coding.title: Ternary Min
coding.objective: int a=3, b=9. (a<b)?a:b gives the minimum. Output?

options: C++ switch doesn't support std::string — use if/else: if (day == "Monday") cout << "Start!";
debugging.answer: C++ switch doesn't support std::string — use if/else: if (day == "Monday") cout << "Start!";
debugging.hint: C++ switch only works with integer types. For string branching, use if/else with == (which works correctly for std::string).

prediction.explanation: n=3 matches case 3: prints C. No break → falls through to case 4: prints D. break → stops. Output: CD.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE OPTIMIZER
teaching.analogy: The Optimizer is the C++ compiler's most powerful subsystem — it sees through every conditional, eliminates dead branches, and reduces your logic to its purest form. Only code that's logically correct AND efficient can defeat it!
teaching.content: THE OPTIMIZER tests ALL Level 2 C++: C++ string == for content, bool type, && and || operators (and/or alternatives), if/else if/else, C++17 init-statements, switch with int/char, [[fallthrough]], ternary operator.

examples.title: Full Level 2 C++ arsenal
examples.code:
✅ C++ string == compares content (not Java!)
✅ Native bool type, boolalpha for output
✅ &&, ||, ! (and, or, not keywords)
✅ if / else if / else — same as C
✅ C++17 if init-statement
✅ switch with int/char only
✅ [[fallthrough]] for intentional fall-through
✅ Ternary ? : with auto type deduction

teaching.funFact: The C++ optimizer (-O2 flag) can transform complex chains of if/else into CPU-level branch prediction hints, lookup tables, or conditional moves. Your readable if/else can compile to highly optimized machine code! ⚡

mcq.q: C++ string s1='hi', s2='hi'. Does (s1 == s2) evaluate to true?
options: Yes — C++ string == is overloaded for content comparison
mcq.explanation: C++ Operator Overloading: std::string overloads == to compare content. (s1 == s2) correctly returns true when both strings have identical characters. This is different from Java where you need .equals().

mcq.q: auto r = (true) ? 5 : 3.14. What type is r?
mcq.explanation: Type Promotion: 5 is int, 3.14 is double. Ternary needs both branches to have compatible types — they're promoted to double (the wider type). r is double.

mcq.q: What output does 'cout << boolalpha << false;' produce?
mcq.explanation: boolalpha Manipulator: After 'cout << boolalpha', booleans print as 'true'/'false' (all lowercase). Without it, they print as 1/0. It affects all subsequent bool output on that stream.

mcq.q: When should you use [[fallthrough]] in a switch?
options: When fall-through is intentional — to document it and suppress the warning
mcq.explanation: Intentional Fall-Through: Use [[fallthrough]] when you deliberately want execution to continue to the next case. It serves as documentation AND tells the compiler it's intentional — suppressing warnings.

mcq.q: C++ 'or' keyword vs '||' — which is correct?
options: Both are valid C++ — 'or' is a keyword alternative
mcq.explanation: Both Valid: The C++ standard includes 'and', 'or', 'not' as keyword alternatives to &&, ||, !. Both are equally valid. Most code uses || because it's more concise and familiar.

coding.title: Boss: String Comparison
coding.objective: C++ way to compare strings — what operator works correctly?
coding.hint: C++ std::string overloads == for content comparison!

coding.title: Boss: Ternary Absolute Value
coding.objective: int n = -8. (n < 0) ? -n : n gives the absolute value. Output?

options: switch case values must be compile-time integer CONSTANTS — expressions like score>=90 are not allowed. Use if/else for range checking.
debugging.answer: switch case values must be compile-time integer CONSTANTS — expressions like score>=90 are not allowed. Use if/else for range checking.
debugging.hint: C++ switch cases must be constant integer expressions known at compile time. score>=90 is a runtime expression — use if/else chains for range-based decisions.

prediction.explanation: 6%2==0 is true. boolalpha makes it print 'true'. 6>10 is false, 6<5 is false → false||false = false → 'false'.
